coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dots from babassu coconut (Orbignya speciosa) biomass: Synthesis, characterization, and toxicity to Daphnia magna

coredata.dc:description:
The present study provides amethodology for the synthesis of carbon dots through hydrothermal carbonization of the babassu coconut (Orbignya speciosa) mesocarp as a precursor matrix. The synthesis parameters (temperature, residence time and% m/v) were studied through a factorial design with the fluorescence intensity as determining factor in choosing the best sample. Particles with diameters in the range of 2.4 - 47.7 nm were obtained, which presented two emission regions, one at approximately 467 nm (
λ
E X
= 370 nm) and the other at approximately 550 nm (
λ
E X
= 470 nm). These carbon dots showed the phenomenon of suppression of fluorescence when in the presence of Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+ and Cd2+ ions, exhibiting good linearity in the fluorescence decay according to the concentration of ions in the medium, indicating potential application as a sensor for these ionic species. Acute toxicity testing with Daphnia magna model demonstrated absence of toxic effect (immobility) of the carbon dots to this aquatic organism bioindicator after 48 h of exposure, even at high concentrations (up to 1000 mg.L−1).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The present study provides amethodology for the synthesis of carbon dots through hydrothermal carbonization of the babassu coconut (Orbignya speciosa) mesocarp as a precursor matrix. The synthesis parameters (temperature, residence time and% m/v) were studied through a factorial design with the fluorescence intensity as determining factor in choosing the best sample. Particles with diameters in the range of 2.4 - 47.7 nm were obtained, which presented two emission regions, one at approximately 467 nm (
λ
E X
= 370 nm) and the other at approximately 550 nm (
λ
E X
= 470 nm). These carbon dots showed the phenomenon of suppression of fluorescence when in the presence of Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+ and Cd2+ ions, exhibiting good linearity in the fluorescence decay according to the concentration of ions in the medium, indicating potential application as a sensor for these ionic species. Acute toxicity testing with Daphnia magna model demonstrated absence of toxic effect (immobility) of the carbon dots to this aquatic organism bioindicator after 48 h of exposure, even at high concentrations (up to 1000 mg.L−1).

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) are discrete spherical carbonaceous nanoparticles with photoluminescent properties [1,2], low toxicity, high photostability, and good biocompatibility [3–6] among other unique physical-chemical characteristics that allow applications in biosensors, optoelectronic devices, drug delivery, bio-images, and light-emitting devices [7–10], etc.

The literature reports several methods to obtain fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, such as chemical oxidation [11], hydrothermal treatment [12–14], microwave [15], plasma treatment [16], pyrolysis [17], solid state reaction method [18], etc. The different synthesis approaches (top-down and bottom-up) include possibilities of different reaction routes. In addition, countless carbon sources can be used as precursors to configure different types of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, with variations in chemical composition and size of the nanoparticles as well as formation of the conjugated π carbon nucleus and functional groups on the surface of these C-dots [19,20].

There are two main theories about the mechanism responsible for fluorescence in carbon dots. One attributes the effect to quantum confinement due to the small size of the particles. However, the more popular theory is based on the π conjugated electronic transitions related to the surface states of primarily oxygenated groups that involve the carbon nucleus [21–28]. These nanoparticles generally have a carbon core with sp2 bonds and surface oxygenated groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and aldehyde) [27], and are usually prepared from precursors whose structure is formed by aromatic rings and several heteroatoms under controlled synthesis conditions [29,30]. This the theory explains variations in photoluminescence (PL) energy even in samples that have similar particle sizes and PL emissions with the same energy in large size distributions [26].

Biomass is a very promising precursor matrix for the synthesis of carbon dots, as a low-cost carbon source, abundantly availability in nature, with rapid regeneration. Moreover, its potential use could have an important environmental aspect by decreasing natural waste. The use of residues as raw material in the development of products for primary use is quite innovative and potentially economical because it adds value to all production chain. All this understanding makes part of the Waste-to-Wealth concept, and aims to promote a future sustainable lifestyle, where the increase in value of the residues has relation with the intrinsic benefits to the environment, and promotes the development of new technologies, ways of subsistence and production [31,32]. Hydrothermal carbonization is a widely adopted synthesis route employed to obtain carbon dots from biomass, due to its ecofriendly character, easy operation, and light reaction conditions [33,34]. Many studies have been reported on the hydrothermal treatment of bio-based renewable precursors [35], including sucrose [36], wheat straw [37], orange juice [38], plant leaves [39], natural fibers [40], chitosan [41], lignin [42], starch [43], and polysaccharides in general [36].

The babassu coconut (Orbignya speciosa) is a Brazilian species quite common in the region known as Mata dos Cocais, a transitional biome, with characteristics of the Amazon Forest, Cerrado, and Caatinga. Babassu could be an excellent biomass source, both for its occurrence and for its composition that allows it to be fully used [44–47]. Due to its amylaceous composition (60% on average), babassu mesocarp (BM) has large amounts of α-glucose in its polymeric chain, which can nucleate to form a dense carbon center with oxygenated functional groups [46,48] presenting great potential as a precursor for the synthesis of C-dots.

The elementary composition of the final product is the result of the synthesis parameters and biological characteristics of the carbonaceous matrix employed. Precursors with amino acids and proteins contribute nitrogen to the composition of the product. Starting materials with the presence of chlorophyll provide in addition to carbon and nitrogen, magnesium in the final product. Biomass sources in general contain essential minerals, such as K, Na, P, and Fe [14,49]. Depending on the treatment steps and the type of synthesis used, the generated C-dot suspension can contain minimal amounts of these elements, which can influence the concentrations of fluorescent particles in the samples. This increases the margin of errors in the interpretation of the results of fluorescence intensity, if the total concentration of the solution is considered as the concentration of fluorescent particles. Thus, it is important to the quantities of these constituents in the environment must be taken into account and methodologies establish to mitigate their effects.

The fluorescence intensity can also be influenced by the presence of some substances that, when added to a fluorescent medium, act as fluorescence suppressors. Some metal ions have this behavior when added to a medium with carbon dots. The suppression of fluorescence competes with the radioactive emission of the same multiplicity as the emitter, limiting its intensity and/or lifetime [50,51]. The phenomenon occurs through a new recombination of the electron/hole pair between the surface of the carbon nanoparticles and the metal ions without light emission. This common process in carbon dots originating from biomass sources changes their fluorescence intensity [52].

The range of application possibilities of C-dots associated with characteristics, such as their high hydrophilicity attributed to superficial polar groups, motivate the need for biosafety studies. However, the amount of research on this for these materials is still limited. Gel-like carbon dots were used for the photocatalytic degradation of water pollutants and showed good biocompatibility and very low cytotoxicity for sea urchin embryos [53]. Ecotoxicity studies with biological models using zebrafish (Danio rerio) [6,54] and Zooplankton (Daphnia magna) [6,54] demonstrated the high biocompatibility and low toxicity of C-dots. However, the study with algae (Chlorella pyrenoidosa, Straphylococcus aureus, and Microcystis aeruginosa) [55,56], found that under certain conditions, C-dots inhibited their development, indicating their greater sensitivity to the exposure of certain types of carbon dots. The rapid and current development of nanomaterials must take into account their effects when discarded or act in the environment. The Daphnia magna model has been widely used to assess the toxicity of nanomaterials and their potential impacts on water quality and environmental risks [57,58]. Toxicity studies make important contributions in the development of new materials to guarantee safety and sustainability.

This work uses babassu coconut biomass, specifically mesocarp, as a carbon source to synthesize carbon nanodots through hydrothermal carbonization. We followed a factorial design to evaluate the best synthesis parameters (residence time, temperature, and m/v – percentage of babassu mesocarp mass in relation to the synthesis volume), which provide the material with better fluorescence intensity. In addition, the realization and characterization of these nanoparticles and the study of their photoluminescence, sensitivity and selectivity properties in the detection of metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, and Cd2+) help to enhance the production chain of the babassu coconut through the development of a promising material with high added value and interesting applications in modern science and technologies. Acute toxicity tests were carried out on D. magna, where we verified an absence of toxicity for carbon dots produced from babassu coconut biomass.

The BM was acquired from the company Babcoal (http://babcoall.blogspot.com/). No pretreatment was performed. Sodium hydroxide P.A. purchased by Synth Ltda (Brazil). All chemical reagents were used as received without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factorial design and synthesis of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: A Box-Behnken design (BBD) was used to evaluate the combination of 3 factors (temperature, time, and concentration of the carbon source) at three levels (−1, 0, +1), consisting of a 33 design with 15 experiments, being 3 central points, as shown in Table 1 . In this factorial design, the evaluated variables were temperature (condition -: 190 °C; condition +: 230 °C), time (condition -: 4 h; condition +: 24 h) and initial babassu mesocarp concentration (condition -: 1% m/v; condition +: 10% m/v). The response of experiment was the fluorescence intensity. The samples were named with the acronym CDM (Carbon Dot of Mesocarp). The experiments were randomly numbered from 1 to 15, according to the software used. This factorial design was operationalized using the Statistica ® software.

In a typical process, the determined mass of babassu mesocarp (1%, 5.5%, and 10%) was suspended in 50 mL of a NaOH 1 mol/L solution. The suspension was taken to an ultrasound bath for 5 min and stirred magnetically for 30 min. Then, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon container with a lid and taken to a stainless-steel reactor (autoclave). The reactor was closed and heated in a muffle furnace according to the predetermined temperature (190 °C, 210 °C, and 230 °C) and residence time (4 h, 14 h, and 24 h), the initial pH was approximately 14. After carbonization, the material was filtered on a 0.22 µm membrane, and dialyzed on a 3.5 kD membrane for 48 h.

To determine the ash content of the samples, the lyophilization process of the solutions with carbon dots was first performed, employing an Enterprise II, Terroni brand lyophilizer. The samples were dripped in liquid nitrogen for freezing and immediately taken to the freeze dryer where they remained for about 48 h, until the sublimation of all the liquid.

Then, thermogravimetric analyses were performed on a TGA/DTA equipment, Netzsch STA449 F3 Jupiter, to determine after dialysis the concentration of solids in the suspension of carbon dots (measure 1) and the ash content of lyophilized samples (measure 2). The measurements were conducted between 25 °C to 200 °C and 25 °C to 1000 °C, respectively, both with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1, in synthetic air. These results are presented in Table 1 and they allowed a tentative determination of the content of carbonaceous material estimated by Eq. (1) proposed. This can provide a more approximate value the of fluorescent carbon concentration, minimizing any errors in the fluorescence measurements.

In each of the factorial design samples, we obtained the contour graph of the excitation spectra in which emission was measured in a Horiba Fluorolog 3 spectrofluorometer. The static fluorescence spectra were performed on a Tecan Infinite Microplate Reader spectrophotometer with excitations recorded at
λ
E X 1
: 370 nm and
λ
E X 2
: 470 nm.

To analyze the morphology of the nanoparticles, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed. TEM images were acquired with JEOL JEM 2100F TEM microscope field-emission gun transmission electron microscope (acceleration voltage 200 kV, Spot Size 2, Alpha Selector 3). Images were obtained in STEM mode under HAADF. The samples were prepared using a few milligrams of the sample, lyophilized, dispersed in isopropyl alcohol and sonicated for 15 min and dropped on a carbon-coated copper grid (ultrathin carbon/holey carbon, 400 mesh copper grid, Ted Pella, Inc.).

The measurements of Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) to estimate the surface charge and particle size were performed in a Zetasizer Nano ZS equipment by Malvern, with a polycarbonate cuvette equipped with gold electrodes coated with beryllium/copper (model DTS 1070).
The lyophilized sample was analyzed by spectroscopy in the infrared region by KBr on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 spectrophotometer.
A Thermo Scientific Multiskan GO ™ Microplate Spectrophotometer was used to obtain the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra.

Chemical surface of CDM-6 sample was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a K-Alpha XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK). Survey and high-resolution spectra were obtained using a Al Kα (1486 eV) source, and 200 and 50 eV pass-energy was used for the measurements, respectively. Three different areas (spot size of 400 µm) were analyzed for statistical purposes and spectra were fitted using the Thermo Advantage software (Version 5.921).

Firstly, nitrate solutions of Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, and Cd2+ with ions concentration of 0.1 mol.L-1, were prepared separately. Subsequently, aliquots of the ions solutions were removed so that 30, 90, 150, 210 and 300 µMols of each of these metallic ions, were obtained. Each aliquot was added to 3.0 mL of the carbon dots solution (CDM 6). Then, they were submitted to ultrasound for 2 min, and after 30 min, the fluorescence intensity each point recorded [18,19]. The static fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Tecan Infinite Microplate Reader spectrophotometer with excitations recorded at 370 nm and 470 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acute toxicity test on Daphnia magna
ce:section.ce:para: The acute toxicity test on D. magna (immobility) assay was based on OECD TG 236 guidelines [59], and NBR ABNT 12,713: 2016 standard [60], with three replications. The neonate organisms, (<24 h) D. magna, were used, and a toxicity test was carried out at the National Nanotechnology Laboratory - LNNano of the Brazilian National Research in Center for Energy and Materials (CNPEM).

Toxicity tests were conducted in 24-well plates with 2 mL of reconstituted water as a diluent, 5 daphnids per well and exposure time of 48 h, with a first reading taken at 24 h of exposure. The following concentrations of carbon dots (sample CDM-6) were evaluated: 1, 10, 100, 500, and 1000 mg.L−1. After the exposure period, the toxicity was analyzed to mobility, and the organisms that were unable to resume swimming under gentle agitation were considered immobile.

According to the literature, babassu mesocarp has an ash content between 3.6% and 6.5% [46,61]. Its heterogeneous composition, associated with the different synthesis parameters adopted in the factorial design, provided conditions to obtain different concentrations of fluorescent particles in each of the synthesized samples. The TGA results of each liquid and lyophilized sample, using Eq. (1), permitted estimation of carbonaceous material content closer to the real one. Table 1 provides these values, which indicate that the CDM-6 sample had a lower concentration of carbonaceous material (0.112% by mass). The other samples were diluted to this concentration, to reduce errors in the concentration of fluorescent nanoparticles in the fluorescence intensity measurements.

To determine the excitation and emission regions of the PL, the contour measurement of the excitation and emission spectra was performed for all samples that are part of the factorial design (see Fig. 1 ). In the regions of warmer colors (green to red), all samples had similar excitation regions approximately 360 and 380 nm (UV) and an emission center between 510 and 540 nm (visible). Especially samples CDM 3, CDM 5, CDM 6 and CDM 10 present a second center with an excitation region between 460 and 480 nm and emission between 540 and 570 nm. Different emission regions may be related to the number of vibrational energy levels in the ground state and may indicate potential structural differences [62]. These two emission and excitation matrices can be related to fluorophores of different superficial functional groups. We assume that they could be present in all samples analyzed in a lower concentration.

The fluorescence intensity of all samples at the two emission centers (EM1:
λ
E X
= 370 nm and EM2:
λ
E X
= 470 nm) is presented in Fig. 2 , which verifies a very clear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of the carbon source used to synthesize the samples. The fluorescence intensity tended to decrease with the increased concentration of the precursor source added during the synthesis. This effect could be related to a higher demand for thermal energy and a longer residence time to decompose the polymers of the carbonaceous matrix and generate sufficient monomers that will participate in the rest of the nucleation and generation of nanoparticles. This incomplete hydrolysis has already been observed in other studies that use biomass to generate other carbon materials by hydrothermal route [63,64].

Figs. 1 and 2 illustrate that samples CDM 5, CDM 6 and CDM 10 presented higher fluorescence intensities. These all had only 1% w/v of mesocarp in their syntheses; thus, the biomass concentration exerted greater effect than the variation of temperature and residence time. According to the data in Fig. 2, the fluorescence intensity in the second emission center (EM2) is more prominent for most samples, thus, the study of the effect of the variables on the fluorescence intensity was carried out with these values. Analysis of the effects of the synthesis parameters, evaluated separately and combined with other effects, is presented in Fig. 3 . The Pareto diagram (see Fig. 3(a)) indicates that the combined effect of temperature and concentration was superimposed on any other individual effect. The quadratic temperature effect is also significant and indicates that the relationship between factor and response follows a curved line. However, the analysis of the effect of temperature under the proposed conditions and the combination of the effects of time and concentration were not significant. The isolated effect of the carbonization time was accentuated in the increase of the fluorescence of the obtained materials.

The conditions for the BBD shown in Table 1 was employed to optimize the variables of carbon dot synthesis process and examine their effects on fluorescence intensity. The results indicated the fluorescence intensity was in the range of 14,821 – 50,641 (a.u.), also CDM-8 sample with the lowest fluorescence intensity, and the CDM-6 sample with the highest fluorescence intensity. The optimal conditions for the synthesis of these fluorescent carbon nanoparticles were obtained at the temperature of 230 °C, residence time of 14 h and mesocarp concentration (m/V) of 1.0. This desirability condition is shown in Fig. 3(b), is befitting with sample CDM 6 (see Table 1) and was predicted by the polynomial model (Eq. (2)), resulted a fluorescence intensity of 45,656.5 (a.u.).

X2: Biomass concentration (m/V).
Note: X1, X2 and X3 in terms of Coded Factors (Table 1). The term X2×3 was not significant, according to the Pareto diagram (Fig. 3(a)).

Analysis of variance (ANOVA) results were demonstrated in Table S1. As can be seen, the effect of proposed model for the fluorescence intensity of babassu mesocarp carbon dots were significant. The F-value (4.8045) and the low p-value (p < 0.05) show that the proposed model was significant, despite the coefficient of determination calculated to have been 86.50%. Also, lack-of-fit for obtained response was insignificant (0.1259). Considering the given results, the square polynomial model (Eq. (2)) was extremely suitable for representing the relationship between variables and responses and there was no need for higher order models [65,66].

According to the proposed model, the time and mesocarp concentration factors were significantly effective in the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots. As can be seen, the fluorescence intensity increases with time to some extent, then starts to decay (Fig. 3(c) and (d)). Studies indicate that the period of greatest carbon conversion occurs during the first 8 h of the synthesis, in addition, the increase of temperature can accelerate the nucleation process of carbon particles [67], which could generate particles without fluorescence, or with less fluorescence under the conditions analyzed. In the comparison between the two, the effect of time is more relevant than temperature when these effects were combined.

Analysis of the combined effects of reaction time and biomass concentration also showed an increasing effect on fluorescence intensity with increasing time to some extent, then decreasing thereafter (Fig. 3(d)), probably for the same reason mentioned previously [67]. In this case, the concentration effect was more relevant than the reaction time effect. The combined effects of mesocarp concentration and temperature (Fig. 3(e)) generally increased fluorescence at lower biomass concentrations and elevated temperatures. Higher substrate concentration can cause incomplete hydrolysis of the precursor, causing an insufficient polymerization of soluble products, decreasing the aggregation and nucleation of the formed carbonaceous particles [67].

The synthesis parameters and the carbon source determine the properties of the generated carbon dots. Therefore, the importance of evaluating these conditions using factorial design. Table 2 shows the comparison of some characteristics of C-dots from different biomass sources synthesized hydrothermally under different conditions.

It is already discussed in the literature that the hydrothermal carbonization of biomass can generate different types of carbon structures depending on the carbonaceous precursor and the synthesis parameters used [35]. For the formation of carbon dots by hydrothermal route, this premise is also crucial for the final structure of these nanoparticles, in the process of formation of the carbon nucleus and its superficial oxygen and/or nitrogen groups, which will influence the bandgap between a surface state and conduction band [5], and lead to the excitation-dependent emission of C-dot, as seen in the results shown in Table 2.

In comparison with the other studies presented in Table 2, our work shows carbon dots with two emission centers (and with two excitation regions). However, the size of particles, in comparison with the cited works, presents higher values than most of them. Probably the diversified composition of the babassu precursor can be related to these phenomena.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CQDs of the working sample

ce:section.ce:para: STEM images showed that the CQDs of the CDM 6 sample contained spherical particles, attributed to the brightest regions, with diameters in the range of 2.4–47.7 nm (Fig. 4 (a)) and an average diameter of about 19.8 nm (blue histogram – Fig. (b)). A large range of sizes may be due to agglomerations of these nanoparticles as illustrated in the DLS (red histogram – Fig. (c)) histogram, ∼ 60 nm, and may be related to their surface charge of approximately −42.6 mV, indicated by the of Zeta Potential measurements. The measurement of particle sizes for the construction of histograms was performed with the aid of ImageJ software, with Gaussian particle size distribution adjustment and with FWHM of 29,6 nm.

The structure of the C-Dots of the CDM-6 sample was characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. As charted in Fig. 4(d), carbon dots display absorption bands in the region of 3710–2980 cm−1, referring to hydroxyl groups present in the starch structure [46]. These functional groups improved the hydrophilicity and stability of nanoparticles in aqueous media. The bands at 2980–2830 cm−1 in the mesocarp spectrum refer to terminal stretches CH2 and CH3 [46]. The band at 1637 cm−1 in the mesocarp corresponds to hydroxyl groups in the starch structure, and appearance of bands linked to CC bonds with no spectrum of carbon dots, and the reduction of existing CH bonds, indicate a dehydration reaction in the starch structure, compatible with the nucleation of unsaturated and aromatic carbon chains that form the structure of the C-dots [63]. The presence of carboxylates in the carbon dots structure is confirmed by the appearance of two bands, one shoulder at 1573 cm−1 referring to asymmetric axial deformation, and the other, an intense band at 1403 cm-1 that comes from the symmetric axial deformation of carboxylates [68].

The bands at 1249, 1157, 1070, and 1033 cm−1 in the mesocarp sample indicate the presence of COC bonds, undoubtedly the glycosidic bonds of the starch chain rings. The more intense bands at 894, 755, and 694 cm−1 in the carbon dots are related to vibrations of monosubstituted aromatic esters and rings [69], possibly resulting from the nucleation process in the formation of the carbon nuclei of the nanoparticles.

The chemical composition of the CDM-6 sample analyzed and estimated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Carbon dots have 55.9% of carbon, 29.1% of oxygen and 15.0% of sodium (Fig. 5 ). Carbon and oxygen are the common elements that form carbon dots structure, while sodium is found due to the use of sodium hydroxide during the sample synthesis. The high oxygen content found at the sample is related to the high functionalization degree of prepared carbon dots, which is confirmed by the high resolution C1s and O1s spectra (Fig. 5) and might be associated to its high colloidal stability and zeta potential value. Sodium might be present on carboxylate groups as soluble contaminants are not expected after samples purification by dialysis. High resolution C1s spectrum showed the presence of 11.3% of CO and 20.4% CO, as it also found by FTIR. High resolution O1s spectrum presented 79.0% of CO and 21.1% of COH groups.

The aqueous solution of the obtained carbon dots had a brownish color in the presence of white light but exhibited a green fluorescence when irradiated by a 365 nm UV lamp (see image in Fig. 6 ). The emission spectra of the CDM 6 sample are presented in Fig. 6(a), with two fluorescence regions. In the EM1 fluorescence center, the
λ
E X
occurred at 370 nm and the emission peak at approximately 467 nm, while in the EM2 fluorescence center, more intense than the first, the
λ
E X
occurred at 470 nm and the emission peak at approximately 550 nm. A weak peak of absorption occurred at approximately 260 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 6(b) - blue spectra), probably derived from π-π * transitions of CC. A wide shoulder observe between approximately 320 and 380 nm can be assigned and n - π * of – C = O transitions, possibly of the superficial groups [70].

The acidification of the medium (Fig. 6(c)) caused a marked decrease in the fluorescence intensity. This phenomenon could be related to the surface state of these nanoparticles under protonation/deprotonation effects. The superficial oxygenated groups seem to have less efficiency for radioactive recombination in an environment with lower pH values [71–73].

Fluorescence suppression is a phenomenon where the fluorescence intensity of the emitting species is decreased by contact with a suppressive agent. Some metallic ions are excellent suppressants for some types of C-dots, probably because they cause electron or energy transfer [52] in the possible formation of a complex between these cations and the oxygenated and negatively charged surface of the quantum carbon dots (static suppression of fluorescence) [74]. The graphs in Fig. 7 (a) presents the decay in the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized C-dots with increased concentration of added metallic ions. The fluorescence spectra are provided in Figure S1. The systematic decrease in emission with the increase in the concentration of metal ions in the samples occurred in both excitation wavelengths (
λ
E X 1
: 370 nm and
λ
E X 2
: 470 nm) and for both metal ions with reasonable linearity as indicated by the ​​ R2 values in each analysis. In Figure S1, a shift in the emission band 1 (
λ E X
: 370 nm) to approximately 467 nm is observed compared to the measurement of the contour fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1), a hypothesis for this effect is that the two types of fluorescence measurements were taken on different equipment.

Of all the metal ions studied in this experiment, iron clearly exhibited the best interaction and suppressive activity, with the greatest fluorescence decay in both emission centers. The R2 values ​​in the two emission regions (R2 EM1 = 0.9800 R2 EM2 = 0.9254) showed, in addition to good sensitivity, a linear decay of fluorescence intensity with the concentration of the added metal, demonstrating that the synthesized carbon dots can efficiently detection iron (III) ions. The Co, Ni, and Cu ions presented interactions slightly lower than iron but were still effective in the two emission regions, with linear variation of the fluorescence intensity at the concentration of the metals studied in the suppression tests (Co - R2 EM1 = 0.9606 and R2 EM2 = 0.9073; Ni - R2 EM1 = 0.9412 and R2 EM2 = 0.8947; Cu - R2 EM1 = 0.8947 and R2 EM2 = 0.9039). The Zn and Cd ions had more discrete and very similar suppression activities, but with good linear correlations between metal concentration and fluorescence intensity (Zn - R2 EM1 = 0.9858 and R2 EM2 = 0.9821; Cd - R2 EM1 = 0.9617 and R2 EM2 = 0.9700).

The mechanism of fluorescence emission is associated with electron transfer between the valence band (HOMO orbitals) and the conduction band (LUMO orbitals) in carbon dots. Thus, in excitation, the lower absorption energy of a free electron in the valence band must be greater than the band gap. In that case, the electron of the surface oxygenated groups could get rid of a covalent bond and would transition from the valence band to the conduction band when excited at 370 nm
(
λ
E X 1
)
and 470 nm
(
λ
E X 2
)
. When relaxing, it would return from the conduction band to the valence band, emitting radiation [75]. Fig. 7(b) shows a schematic illustration of this process.

The added metallic ions cause the surface oxygenated groups of the C-dots to interact with the d orbitals of these ions. The electron excited in the carbon dot donor groups are partially transferred to the d orbitals of cations that are not fully filled. The fluorescence extinction mechanism is influenced by the availability of these empty or semi-filled d orbitals. When metallic ions coordinate with surface oxygenated groups in C-dots, they promote a non-radioactive recombination of the electron-hole pair, by an effective process of transferring electrons or energy [19,52,76,77], as illustrated in the scheme in Fig. 7(b).

Thus, the different interactions between these carbon dots and the metal ions studied, which provided suppression of fluorescence in different variations of intensity, as shown in Figure S1, must be related to the availability of empty or semi-filled d orbitals of these metal cations, influencing the coordination between metal ions and phenolic hydroxyl groups or other surface oxygenated groups of these nanoparticles [75,77–79]. Thus, Fe3+ ([Ar] 3d5), which has greater availability of semi-filled d orbitals, presents greater affinity and in a more effective chelation process with the oxygenated surface of the carbon dots, interacting better than Zn2​​+ ([Ar] 3d10) and Cd2+ ([Kr] 4d10) which have their d orbitals completely filled. The other metallic ions studied follow this same trend, according to the filling of their d orbitals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Suppression of fluorescence in reconstituted water
ce:section.ce:para: The suppression of fluorescence was also analyzed in a solution that carbon dots were added to a medium called “reconstituted water”, for the cultivation of D. magna, prepared with the following salts: CaCl2•2H2O, MgSO4•7H2O, KCℓ, and NaHCO3, according to ABNT NBR 12,713:2016 [60].

Figure S3 graphs the dissenting fluorescence intensity of the C-dots in reconstituted water with increased concentration of added metallic ions. The fluorescence spectra are in Figure S2, and the changes were not significant.

The results are similar to the previous ones performed without reconstituted water, presenting good sensitivity and linear variation of the fluorescence intensity with the concentration, with coherent values ​​of R2 for all the analyzes. Therefore, the C-dots efficiently detected ions, even in saline medium such as reconstituted water. However, some variations are observed in relation to the previous assay. The decrease in fluorescence intensity for Fe3+was less. A precipitation of the iron ions in the saline medium decreased the concentration of Fe3+ions in the medium and, thus the interaction between these and the carbon dots, reducing the slope of the decay of the fluorescence intensity in relation to the previous test in aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absence of acute toxicity to Daphinia magna
ce:section.ce:para: In toxicity studies with D. magna, no acute toxicity for carbon dots was observed after exposure for 48 h, even at high concentration (1000 mg.L−1). These results demonstrated the absence of a toxic effect for this aquatic organism and are a good indicator of environmental safety of these materials. To analyze the internalization capacity of nanomaterials, fluorescence microscopy images of D. magna were obtained after exposure to carbon dots. It is possible to observe in Figure S4(b) a fluorescence in Daphnia's intestine after ingestion of the fluorescent carbon nanoparticles. Therefore, these nanoparticles could be candidates for fluorescence images in future bio-imaging experiments for biological and environmental applications.

However, studies have reported that carbon dots produced from citric acid and ethylenediamine [6,80] caused oxidative stress with inhibition of photosynthesis and retarded growth of phytoplankton (Scenedesmus obliquus) with EC50 74.8 mg.L−1 in 96 h . In that study, the material did not affect the swimming and feeding of Zebrafish at a dosage above 200 mg L−1, but it caused low toxicity for D. magna, with the EC50 of 97.5 mg.L−1 [6,81]. Other factors such as the structures of carbon nanoparticles and the medium of exposure also significantly influenced the concentrations that cause toxicity in Daphnias.

Studies with fullerenes (C60) [82,83] and carbon nanotubes (NTCs) [84,85] found a LC50 values ​​between 2.8–4.45 mg.L−1 (for D. pulex species) and 23.6–36.3 mg.L−1 (for D. magna species) for NTCs, and 0.2 to> 50 mg.L−1 for C60. The solubilization of fullerenes in tetrahydrofuran and water registered a LC50 of 0.8 mg.L−1 and > 35, respectively. In the present study, CDM 6 sample did not exhibit toxicity to D. magna (up to 1000 mg.L−1), which reveals a positive factor compared to the toxicity of other carbon nanomaterials and even other carbon dots. The difference in the toxicity of these carbon nanomaterials must take into account factors such as the structure and size of these nanoparticles, dispersion (medium), surface functional groups, surface charge, preparation methods, and carbon source [81].

Babassu coconut mesocarp is a suitable source of biomass for the synthesis of carbon dots, which were effectively obtained as demonstrated in the TEM images (with an average diameter of 19.8 nm) and by emissions in the fluorescence spectroscopy (EM1: 467 nm; EM2: 550 nm). Factorial design proved to be efficient to decide the best synthesis parameters for obtaining C-dots with higher fluorescence intensity. The CDM 3, CDM 5, CDM 6, and CDM 10 samples exhibited the highest fluorescence intensities among the studied samples, elucidating that the combination of temperature and concentration factors exerts the most influence on the selection of higher fluorescence intensity. The CDM 6 sample (Temp. = 230 °C; Time = 14 h; m/v = 1%) had the best combined effect of the three synthesis parameters studied for the generation of carbon dots with greater fluorescence intensity in the two emission regions studied. The fluorescence of C-dots can be suppressed in the presence of the Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+ and Cd2+ ions in aqueous medium. The carbon dots produced do not present acute toxicity to D. magna up to the concentration of 1000 mg L−1, demonstrating that these materials are not toxic for this model organism, suggesting a low risk for contamination of aquatic environments.
